data.title: DRLL: Worst House On A Bad Street
data.description:
Introduction

Energy-related ETFs have not performed well over the last year. This probably comes as no great surprise, given the near 30% decline in oil prices. The subject of this Strive Energy U.S. ETF, (DRLL) shows every indication of being the worst house on a bad street. It is the worst performing ETF in this sector, a trend that hasn't varied since its inception several years ago. Some of this underperformance may be due to the fact that DRLL carries a much higher expense ratio in comparison to similarly structured funds.

It is worth noting the sector itself has also seriously underperformed the SP 500 ETF (SPY) but substantially outperformed the U.S. Oil Fund (USO), which tracks futures in the underlying commodity.

DRLL was initiated to encourage companies to put excellence ahead of other "stakeholder" type considerations. Its co-founder, Vivek Ramaswamy, is an outspoken and enthusiastic capitalist and has actively used his platform as a business leader, candidate for President, and now candidate for the governorship of Ohio to argue against ESG/DEI and other non-shareholder friendly considerations.

One drag on DRLL's market appeal may actually be related to Mr. Ramaswamy's unabashed conservative political leanings and anti-ESG stance on corporate governance. Despite the anti-ESG fanfare with which Ramaswamy launched the fund before beginning his political career, current management under new CEO, Mathew Cole has deemphasized this strident language, suggesting that Strive needed to "recalibrate its woke meter." It also has deleted proxy voting guidelines from its current prospectus. The actual distinction between old DRLL and new DRLL may be a bit qualitative, as the company still has some very assertive, activist language on its website-

Proactively engaging with management teams and boards, Strive aims to unlock value in the U.S. energy sector by mandating companies to focus on excellence.

The thesis for owning DRLL

President Trump has strongly refocused American energy policy toward the production of domestic oil and gas. He views this as being imperative for our national energy security and for export revenue to other countries that lack our resources. There are some disconnects in my view between this goal and his relentless determination to drive down energy prices, but so far production has remained high and prices have declined, so chalk up a win for the administration and, of course, for drivers of Ford Super Duty F-150s.

DRLL's top ten holdings are all well-known American companies, including sector giants like Chevron (CVX) and Exxon Mobil (XOM). To be considered for inclusion in DRLL a stock must be a member of the Bloomberg U.S. 1500 index.

Below, the company puts forward their investment thesis for the American energy sector, noting strong tailwinds for the sector, and a commitment to using their voice to drive excellence and cost efficiency to create value. It sounds good. From my time in the industry, I can tell you there is already a relentless focus on excellence, cost efficiency, and value creation. But one more voice can't hurt. I guess.

This list reads like a "Who's Who" of the American energy cohort, although many of these companies are beginning to have an international footprint as their U.S.-based assets start to age out. An example would be EOG Resources (EOG) operations in Trinidad and Tobago.

A potential catalyst for DRLL

Unless you've been on Mars the last few days, news of the American removal of Venezuela's dictator Maduro and the subsequent takeover of the country's oil assets by the U.S. has reached your ears. By all reports, and it's entirely logical, the country's oil fields and physical assets are in a state of extreme disrepair and neglect. Mismanagement by the country's leadership for the past 2.5 decades has resulted in most leading Western oil companies and service suppliers withdrawing. Chevron is the only company remaining on the ground and producing and exporting Venezuela's crude.

Estimates for the country's ability to produce oil amount to some 3.5 mm BOPD out of a reserve base of over 300 billion bbls of proved reserves. Today's output of around 1 mm BOPD is a far cry from that level. There is likely no quick fix. RBN notes in a blog post that it will likely take tens to hundreds of billions in capital investment and possibly a decade to restore Venezuela's production to former levels.

All of this spells opportunity for the companies in the DRLL portfolio, many of whom have previously operated in the country. Should they be induced to return, it would probably be on a very profitable basis. Quoting the RBN post-

With today’s oil industry focused on capital discipline and shareholder returns, it’s hard to see many international companies committing that kind of money without very strong guarantees. The bottom line is it will likely take years for real change, and there is still much chaos and uncertainty.

One aspect that could result in a quicker uptick in Venezuelan production is the introduction of wide-scale fracking. According to a paper presented at the 2023 Unconventional Resources Technology Conference-URTEQ, the La Luna reservoir could be very prospective for frac-stimulation. That represents the same sort of step-change that could rapidly accelerate daily output, in a similar fashion to how it did in the U.S.

Risks

An investment in DRLL is subject to the same risks of oil price fluctuations that impact the companies that comprise DRLL's portfolio. Some of this inherent risk is tempered by the strength of the companies in which DRLL invests, but the fund's modest 2.99% yield may not offset a "dead money" risk. Yield seekers should probably look elsewhere for investments that fit a dividend income profile.

Your takeaway

I am not historically a big fan of ETFs, as I think better returns are available through stock picking by virtue of rigorous analysis. Speaking directly to the opportunity set that DRLL provides, I don't find it compelling. The high expense ratio is several times that of competing energy oriented funds. As an example, the Vanguard Energy Index Fund, (VDE) has a structure very similar to DRLL, and has a much lower expense ratio and a slightly higher yield.

Accordingly, I rate DRLL as a hold. If you're in it, I don't see a risk of loss outside what I've noted above. That said, I certainly don't see a catalyst for it to outperform and provide much in the way of capital appreciation that would make a strong case for owning it. That's not to say that in a bull case for oil where the DRLL constituents rally, DRLL won't tag along. But I think it's the best it can do, and with the asymmetric opportunity that a repricing of oil would bring, I think there are better opportunities in today's market.

data.title: Athabasca Oil: The Peak Price May Be Near
data.description:
Athabasca Oil (ATHOF) is primarily a thermal oil producer. But it is a small one. Generally, the smaller players have higher costs than the larger ones like Cenovus Energy (CVE). For the time being, the startup of the Trans Mountain Expansion pipeline has increased thermal profits by keeping the discount from light oil historically low. How long that will last is anyone's guess. Just about all producers I follow, including this one, have begun production expansion projects that in the long run will bring back the discount to historical levels. Furthermore, the political uncertainty marked by threats of trade wars and tariffs is threatening a North American recession. So, this may be the time to sell this one and head towards a larger producer like Cenovus Energy to ride out whatever will happen.

Finances

Furthermore, the financial strategy is a bit on the risky side. There is enough political uncertainty that an investor should demand stellar finances. This is especially true for small companies.

(Note: Athabasca Oil is a Canadian producer that reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted.)

Management has decided that the long-term debt shown above is evidently good enough because the company now returns 100% of its cash flow to shareholders (one way or another).

But thermal production is a discounted product compared to light oil. Any recession or worse could widen that discount to effectively eliminate the cash flow generated by production.

That is an especially potent threat because this production is not considered particularly low cost. The cost of production is decent enough to be competitive. But that means that one has to think about the bottom of the oil and gas cycle (and how exactly this company makes it to the next recovery).

It would be far better for investors, and less risky, to repay all of that debt and then build the cash balance a whole lot in preparation for what may come next.

Earnings

The earnings at this point are darn good. Thermal earnings as a whole have not been this good in some time.

As shown above, the excellent cash flow would actually allow for a quick paydown. This company's thermal production has relatively long-lived wells with low decline rates. Additionally, any thermal project has a large upfront cost that generates a depreciation component to protect the cash flow (for a very long time). Therefore, the thermal business will cash flow decently even when the company loses money.

But as noted before, the cash flow issue comes from the discount price for light oil declining to widen that discount. The operating netback right now can easily handle the debt balance. But there is no assurance that will continue if oil prices continue to weaken. Historically, this company has had extremely volatile results. That is what makes this unsuitable to hold during all of this uncertainty.

As can be seen from the results above, management has retired a fair amount of stock (lately) rather than repaying the debt. But that makes this management unafraid of financial risk. That is a very big deal when evaluating a management and hence the investment idea.

Stock Price

I wrote for the last article that the peak stock price may be near. Generally, investors often have a lot of time to get out while making a decent profit. That seems to be the case now.

Clearly the stock price has done rather well over the last few years. But for me, the current political uncertainty would have me evaluating exit opportunities at the current time.

My own belief is that the stock is getting to the point where it is no longer responding to good news. This seems to be happening with a fair number of companies I cover. If it keeps up, we will have that periodic cyclical downturn that occurs with this industry even though the industry never really came back into favor. But that is just the way it is sometimes.

Buy Cenovus Energy

When uncertainty abounds as it does now, it is best to move to larger companies until that uncertainty fades.

Cenovus Energy is a company that I have long followed. The debt is investment grade, and the company has some refining to provide some vertical integration. This places the company in a better position than is the case here to survive a cyclical downturn.

Additionally, the company is far larger and therefore has a bit more bargaining power for its pricing when selling its production. You need every little advantage you can get during a cyclical downturn. I also happen to believe that Cenovus is currently undervalued. I am not sure that is the case here given the appreciation of the stock price from very low levels.

Summary

Athabasca Oil is a company that has some darn good results right now. But the stock price would appear to be ignoring those results. That "ignoring" seems to be spreading and is a result that is beyond the control of management. It may be a sign that a cyclical top is either approaching or has passed. Oftentimes, that is the first clue investors get that it is time to end the investment unless they want to hold through the downcycle.

The current financial situation and business environment (in terms of selling prices) look pretty good. But the market tends to look ahead. Right now, the market does not like what it sees.

Therefore, this stock is likely a strong sell at this point. If one wants to maintain a presence in the thermal business, then a conditional buy would be Cenovus Energy.

One thing I find with professional investors that consistently make money is that they buy when their criteria are met and they sell when those criteria are met. They do not care if the stock goes down after they buy unless the story changes materially (and they do have an exit strategy if things go wrong). Nor do they care if the stock continues to appreciate after they sell.

Instead, professional investors have found a way to make money consistently using whatever strategy they developed, and they are constantly improving that strategy.

On the website, I often get questions about the stock going down after a purchase or the stock going up after a sale. But this indicates that the initial research was not comprehensive enough because a stock price movement is causing doubt about buying and selling decisions.

Yet the professionals make money even in risky situations by being gone before a bankruptcy or severe downturn. They make varying amounts of money on an investment that many on the website hold too long (and then write to me about what could have been).

While the situation continues, I often get comments about being able to make more money (because). But when the downturn happens, the people that cashed in their profits, even if way too early, are way ahead of those that held too long and saw their profits disappear. This is where an investing strategy (and sticking to it) is so important. Making money consistently is more important than getting every last penny in each situation.

Risks

The current political uncertainty could be replaced by something constructive and permanent (like a written trade treaty between Canada and the United States). This may allow the current rally to continue.

However, future commodity prices are both volatile and low visibility. A severe and sustained downturn could materially change the outlook of the company. Therefore, even if something constructive and permanent happened between Canada and the United States, it still may not be worth the risk to continue to hold the stock.

Athabasca is a competitive producer. But it is not low cost. However, there may be some add-on projects that could lower the cost of its production in the future to change its competitive position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: If I Could Only Buy 3 REITs For The Next Year
data.description:
One of my followers here on Seeking Alpha recently asked me:

What REITs would you buy if you could only pick 3 of them for the next year?

I am generally not a big fan of such questions because I don’t think that it is possible to make 1-year predictions. Anything can happen over such a short time period, and I don’t pretend to have a crystal ball. My guess isn’t any better than yours.

However, if I had to answer this question and make such a prediction, I would focus on REITs (VNQ) with clear near-term catalysts for potential upside. This would at least increase the odds of being right, even though I still wouldn’t want to invest based on such a short time horizon.

These would be my three picks:

NewLake Capital Partners, Inc. (NLCP)

President Trump just signed an executive order initiating the process to reschedule cannabis as a less dangerous, Schedule III drug, and as a result of this, our cannabis REIT, NewLake Capital Partners, is now surging in value.

The reason why it is surging so much is that this rescheduling will greatly improve the credit quality of its tenants.

As a Schedule I drug, in line with heroin and LSD, cannabis companies were heavily punished by the IRS. They couldn't deduct business expenses such as payroll, marketing, rent, utilities, etc., and as a result, they were commonly paying effective tax rates of 60-80%.

This made it incredibly difficult even for the largest and most sophisticated operators to remain profitable.

But this rescheduling to a Schedule III drug would allow these companies to deduct all these expenses, which would lead to an immediate drop in taxes and a massive surge in profitability. It would lead to greater free cash flow generation, stronger balance sheets, and easier access to capital as well.

Not only that, but I think that this will pave the way for other favorable regulatory changes. As an example, President Trump has shown support for the SAFER Banking Act, which would provide easier banking access to cannabis companies. He has also shown support for the STATES Act, which would decriminalize cannabis and allow States to decide.

So this was the first catalyst, but likely not the last one. This is a rapidly growing sector, with 88% of U.S. adults supporting at least medical use, and this is now increasingly being reflected at the policy level:

It is a major milestone for NLCP, as it would greatly reduce its tenant risk going forward, which is the main reason why it is so cheaply valued.

Even following the recent surge, the stock is still trading at just 8x FFO, an estimated 38% discount to NAV, and an 11% dividend yield, safely covered with an 82% payout ratio, which is at the low end of their 80-90% targeted range. This low valuation is especially intriguing when you consider that the REIT is net cash positive (more cash than debt).

This low valuation would make sense to some extent if you expected major lease defaults and uncertain future releasing prospects.

NLCP has certainly been facing such issues. As an example, YR Wellness, accounting for 5.9% of NLCP's total rent, is undergoing financial restructuring and has ceased rent payment starting in August. NLCP has applied over $500,000 in deposits toward rent, and at quarter end, the REIT still had a little over $400,000 in its security deposit left to apply to Q4 rent. Another tenant, Revolutionary Clinics, went bankrupt and ultimately vacated its sole property owned by NLCP. The property is now being marketed for lease to a new tenant. C3 Industries is also unable to pay full rent right now, and NLCP is marketing one of its leased properties for sale. The few analysts who cover NLCP forecast a drop in AFFO per share of 6-7% next year as a result of these tenant difficulties.

So this explains the ultra-low valuation.

But conditions could soon materially improve for all cannabis companies, and this is why NLCP's stock is surging.

Like you, I generally don't like to buy a stock after it has recently risen. I would rather buy it after a recent dip to take advantage of market volatility.

But in this case, it isn't too late, in my opinion.

The first leg of this rally was the result of pre-announcement speculation, which we did not want to participate in.

Now that the announcement has been made, the stock has surged 15%, but even then, it is still down 25% over the past year:

If conditions really improve as we predict, I would expect this recovery to continue over the coming year.

This, of course, does not mean that NLCP is now without risks.

This remains a more speculative REIT given its small size and focus on cannabis cultivation facilities.

But overall, NLCP has done a great job of mitigating risks by remaining net cash positive, maintaining a lower payout ratio, and focusing on properties located in limited license states, leased primarily to above-average operators with large scale, better access to capital, and greater profitability:

Now we expect the credit quality of these tenants to start to improve, which should reduce lease defaults going forward and improve their future releasing prospects as well.

Long term, we think that NLCP could enjoy 50-100% upside, and you earn an 11% dividend yield while you wait. I will try to set up an interview with the CEO of the company, so let me know if you have any questions for him.

Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc. (IIPR)

Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR) is NLCP’s bigger and older brother.

It also focuses on cannabis cultivation facilities, but it came public 5 years earlier and managed to grow into a much bigger REIT with a $1.4 billion market cap.

NLCP IIPR
Market Cap $0.3 billion $1.4 billion

IIPR is set to benefit in the same way as NLCP from the cannabis rescheduling. Therefore, the investment thesis is fairly similar here. The REIT is heavily discounted because its tenants are struggling and some are defaulting on their leases, but this catalyst could greatly improve its tenants' credit quality, mitigating this risk going forward. However, the main difference between IIPR and NLCP is that IIPR is even cheaper but also riskier.

NLCP IIPR
FFO Multiple 8x 7x
Dividend Yield 11% 15%

There are two reasons why it is riskier.

First, IIPR has some leverage on its balance sheet. Its LTV is low at about 10%, but it then also uses some preferred equity on top of that. In comparison, NLCP is more conservative with a net cash position (more cash than debt).

Second, its average asset quality is lower in my opinion. It has greater exposure to weaker tenants and has historically suffered more lease defaults. I think that this is because the REIT went through a period of explosive growth from 2017 'until 2021 when its cost of capital was very low, and it could earn huge spreads by acquiring new properties. This pushed the REIT to acquire huge volumes of assets over a short time period, and I suspect that its diligence standard then suffered, and it ended up acquiring some bad assets along the way.

But if cannabis now gets rescheduled, and cannabis operators see their profitability surge, this should greatly reduce tenant credit issues going forward. And given that the REIT has a bit of leverage and is more heavily discounted than NLCP, it has even more upside potential.

The thesis is similar, and which you favor depends on your tolerance for risk.

Safehold Inc. (SAFE)

Finally, I would consider opening a position in Safehold.

It is the only publicly listed REIT that focuses on ground leases. These assets have extremely long durations with leases of up to 99 years with preset rents and limited rent escalations. As a result of this, their values are very sensitive to movements in interest rates, and since SAFE is leveraged, its equity is even more sensitive than its underlying assets.

This caused its valuation to crash as interest rates surged in recent years:

But if, like me, you think that interest rates are likely to decline in the near term, then SAFE could present significant upside potential, potentially already in 2026. Its share price would need to double just to return halfway to its previous peak.

Why do I expect interest rates to decline?

The Fed cut interest rates 3 times in 2024, another 3 times in 2025, and it is widely expected to keep cutting in 2026 as a result of the weakening labor and consumer markets.

Just consider that we had as many job layoff announcements in 2025 as in 2009 during the great financial crisis:

I think that this is largely the result of the AI revolution, and therefore, I expect a lot more layoffs in 2026. I also think that many of these people will struggle to find a new job, as most companies are just now starting to properly implement AI tools, which will further increase their efficiency, reducing their near-to-mid-term labor needs.

This could lead to a painful transition process with rising unemployment and a slowing economy, which would ultimately also be reflected in lower inflation (especially labor) and lower interest rates across the entire curve.

I think that AI is the biggest deflationary force of the century, and if you agree with that, you may want to invest in long-duration assets, such as ground leases, which still remain heavily discounted today.

SAFE is essentially a leveraged bet on lower interest rates, with potential for 100%+ upside from here. You earn a 5.2% dividend yield while you wait.

Closing Note

These REITs enjoy strong near-term catalysts that could push them a lot higher already in 2026.

But remember what I wrote in the introduction of this article. I don’t think that it is possible to make 1-year predictions, so take this with a grain of salt. The upside could happen in 3 months or in 3 years. But while we wait, we earn a high, steady income from our real estate investments. That’s the beauty of being a High-Yield Landlord.

data.title: International Cannabis Expansion Gives Tilray Positive 2026 Outlook
data.description:
Tilray (TLRY) reported Q2-2026 Financial Performance last Thursday. Revenues increased and net loss improved. International cannabis sales were a key synergy for the improvement. Tilray also reaffirmed its commitment to entering US cannabis market once rescheduling is complete.

Tilray’s stock price recently underwent a reverse split and has still not recovered. The cannabis sector is on the downtrend after investors took profits over the recent news of rescheduling. Tilray’s stock is down 25% over the last year. It is down 17% since I covered the company last month on Seeking Alpha.

I continue my rating of a Hold. Tilray is currently undervalued and expects headwinds from international sales for 2026. The company is well positioned to enter US cannabis markets and already has a large operating footprint in the US with its craft beer and wellness segments. The cannabis sector should get its steam back after rescheduling occurs and new synergies appear.

Q2-2026 Results

Tilray reported $217.5 million in revenue, a 3% increase YoY and a 4% increase QoQ. Gross profit was $57.5 million, an 8% decrease YoY and no change QoQ. Gross margin was 26% versus 30% YoY and 27% QoQ.

Cannabis revenue was $67.5 million, a 3% increase YoY. International sales increased 36% YoY and 51% QoQ. Canadian adult usage cannabis increased 6% YoY. Wholesale revenue was down YoY. Beverage revenue was $50.1 million, representing a 20% decrease YoY. Wellness revenue was $14.6 million. Distribution revenue, including Tilray Pharma, reported historical revenue at $85.3 million, an increase of 26% YoY. Tilray Pharma includes additional international sales.

Net loss was $44.9 million compared to a net loss of $85.3 million YoY and a net loss of $0.3 million QoQ. Cash and ST investments increased to $291.6 million. The increase was due to ATM offerings. Total debt is $264.83 million.

Tilray gave positive outlook for 2026 and expects international sales to continue to drive growth. Current market consensus on the company’s Q3 revenues is $203.8 million, an increase YoY and a decrease QoQ.

Tilray leads its peers in terms of revenue and total assets. Village Farms leads the pack in terms of stock price increase. SNDL, Canopy Growth, Village Farms, and Tilray have firm US entry strategies. All of the companies have international cannabis sales. The rally from the news of rescheduling has subsided and stock prices are down except for Village Farms. Since rescheduling has not occurred yet, international markets offer all of the Canadian cannabis LPs the opportunity for revenue growth.

Investment Strategy and Risk

Tilray is at low risk of poor financial performance. The company is seeing increased revenues and financial performance from international sales. The company’s debt is manageable. US rescheduling of cannabis will bring additional benefits for the company.

A Hold rating for Tilray comes at a moderate to high risk. The stock price has not recovered from the reverse split and the sector is turning weak again. The stock price is likely to drop further before seeing higher price channels. Although Tilray’s financial performance continues to improve, it may not affect the stock price direction.

Conclusion

Tilray reported strong financial performance for Q2-2026. The company is benefiting from strong international sales. Revenues are increasing and net loss has improved. Tilray is positioned to enter US cannabis markets once rescheduling is complete. The company’s stock price has not recovered from the reverse split and it is now undervalued. I continue my rating of a Hold.

data.title: 5 Things That Won't Happen In 2026 (The Alpha Of Inertia)
data.description:
Introduction

Well, 2026 has started, and I want to wish all of my readers a happy new year. As a part of this, I wish everyone fruitful and profitable investments. This is why I want to start this year with an article that will expand the outlook I have recently shared by taking a different approach. I want to focus on 5 things that, as I state in the title, won't happen or, at least, have a very low chance of materializing. You will find events that go from behavioral patterns to structural macroeconomic trends.

My 2026 Outlook So Far

Back in December, I first wrote about "The 3% World", where I tried to put together the data that show me why 2% inflation is far from being a reality.

My second step was shared when my 2026 market outlook was published, where I argued that a favorable setup for the "S&P 493" was forming. The core idea is that earnings growth is going to broaden across some sectors that will benefit from productivity improvements due to the AI revolution. This, of course, is not a trend that affects every company at the same time, and this is why I then dug deeper into the matter and wrote about my sector winners and losers. My view is that Industrials and Utilities will outperform, while Energy and Real Estate will probably lag the market (Consumer Discretionary could as well).

But this brought me to one final piece on a single stock: Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B). The focus here was on the manufacturing assets that Berkshire owns, which made me debate with my readers whether investing in Buffett's creature could be an even more effective way to play the long XLI/XLU thesis.

I was thankful for the comments on this article, and I have to acknowledge that former Seeking Alpha analyst Briar reminded us all that Charlie Munger used to say that investing is about finding great companies and then doing nothing apart from owning them. In Berkshire's case, we can gain exposure to many manufacturing gems of the American economy that are not publicly traded, with the benefit of investing in an extremely efficient capital allocator that can find among its holdings the cheapest source of capital and redirect it to the holdings that, at the moment, present a higher ROIC (you can see, for example, what Berkshire did with BNSF).

But then, two weeks ago, in the news feed of SA, I read how TS Lombard approached the new year, sharing their satirical take on 2026. I chose to expand their perspective, picking the 5 things I believe won't happen in 2026 and offering the data to support my view to help my readers find a few strong picks.

1. You Won't Cancel Your Netflix Subscription

This starts from a personal experience. Two years ago, I canceled my Netflix (NFLX) subscription as I was trying to reduce my expenses. Three months later, my wife signed back in (and I agreed). As a matter of fact, I was among the 50% of subscribers that cancel Netflix and are back within 6 months. The reality is that some subscriptions don't belong to discretionary spending anymore. They are part of our daily life, and they also represent small monthly amounts, which, psychologically, could also represent meaningless expenses while offering us a nice service as a treat. Not all subscriptions are alike. When consumers have to be cost-conscious, they might drop a few streaming platforms, but it is really hard to do without Netflix because of the huge scale and variety of content it now offers compared to all other competitors. It would be nice to hear in the comments how we spent our entertainment time during the holidays. I am willing to bet that Netflix will come out as the winner.

It is true that not all consumers are doing well. However, even though lower-income households are feeling pressured, we have to admit that consumer spending is proving to be highly resilient. This makes me think once again that our spending habits are truly hard to change unless we are forced to. As a result, we are seeing high consumer spending while personal savings are below their pre-pandemic levels and just 30% above the 2016 print (the cumulative inflation for the same decade is above 35%). This means that people save less as long as they can keep their daily comforts. Surely, the fact that disposable income has grown above inflation in recent months has helped this trend.

There is, however, one important distinction to make. As the Boston FED pointed out a few months ago, consumer spending has been mainly supported by higher-income consumers, who still have a lower credit card debt level than the pre-pandemic levels. On the other hand, lower-income households now carry a lot more debt compared to 2019. The aggregate credit card debt by income group figure that the Boston Fed released also shows that during the pandemic, credit card debt decreased by 15% for low-income consumers, by 20% for middle-income consumers, and by 25% for high-income consumers. While the first two groups saw their debt rapidly increase as the pandemic ended, the last group seems to have used some of its excess savings accumulated during the pandemic to pay off its debt. Right now, this cohort has lower credit card debt compared to 2019, and this leaves room for further spending.

So, it seems that when people talk of a K-shaped economy, there is data to confirm this. This is why we are also seeing dollar stores perform well. But when it comes to Netflix, we keep seeing that it is not considered a discretionary expense. This is why I have recently argued that Netflix has already won.

This first point brings us to a few extra considerations. For a modern household, Netflix is not only Netflix; it is basically necessary. There are also a few other extremely sticky subscriptions that won't be canceled. The first one is Amazon Prime (AMZN). Then I would also consider Costco's membership (COST). They are both subscription programs with a renewal rate above 90%.

We could throw in another name: Apple (AAPL). We have all been surprised by Apple's outstanding resilience last year, with the stock barely lagging the market, although its growth rates have been quite low. Although what I am saying might not apply to many low-income consumers, the iPhone is at the front edge of an incredibly sticky ecosystem that consumers only reluctantly abandon. The iPhone 17 is finally renewing enthusiasm around the product, and we have heard for several months after the release that the new lineup is breaking Apple's records. While Apple might be disappointing in the AI race, it surely is capitalizing on this new technological development by leveraging its massive control over devices and hardware. And we all know that Apple is not just a device designer and manufacturer; it is also an incredibly profitable subscription service business.

Summing things up, I want to say that this year, the core subscription moat of the companies I mentioned above won't be wiped away. Right now, Netflix is in a dip, and Amazon still trades at a reasonable valuation (I believe Amazon is the next candidate to do what Google did last year). Costco has also been trading sideways for over a year. Apple is at high multiples, but if iPhone 17 sales surprise, the stock might move up close to $300. So, I think all of these stocks have a big investment case based on the fact that their economics won't change.

2. Washington Won't Tighten Its Belt

With the U.S. national debt continually growing, there has been a lot of talk about fiscal austerity and the federal budget. We heard that tariffs would help reduce the national debt. But, in truth, I see no appetite for a belt-tightening policy, especially with midterm elections coming. Now, tariffs as a share of federal revenues increased, and they now stand between 8 and 9%.

However, last year, the federal government's expenses exceeded its revenues by $458B. Compared to the previous year, there is a decrease in the deficit of $167B, but it is still a deficit. Federal revenues did increase, but so did government spending. The national deficit decreased by $41B. The decrease in the national deficit is noteworthy and will be applauded by everyone who wants the budget to be under control, but, unfortunately, it is a small amount compared to the order of magnitude of the federal budget. By the way, in the last 50 years, we have seen the U.S. government report a surplus only 4 times. We should also know that the last time a surplus was reported was 24 years ago (2001). So, at the end of 2025, the U.S. national debt increased again by another $2.2T to reach the record amount of $37.6T. The Congressional Budget Office (CBO) also wrote recently that its forecasts lead it to believe that the U.S. will probably increase its debt by almost $2T per year for the next decade.

Structural deficits seem thus to be a constant of federal budgets. Now, reversing spending trends is not difficult only for consumers, but it is also an immense challenge for governments, since public investments become really embedded in the economy of a country. Moreover, the recent geopolitical changes we have witnessed in the post-pandemic era set the tone for the end of the "small government" era, supporting instead the thesis that government capital is creating a floor as it is directed both to internal expenses (Social Security, monthly benefits, federal expenses) and to national priorities such as defense and AI dominance. Fiscal austerity, as JP Morgan shows, has been left behind across the world.

In this environment, I see it as very hard for inflation to be back at 2% (The 3% World: The Bond Market Tells What The FED Won't Say [But Markets Don't Care]). Liquidity is everywhere, and government spending is fueling demand, making the economy hot. If Uncle Sam keeps spending, companies that are linked to structural projects and programs and that can win big government contracts become a very interesting play. This is why I have argued that being long on industrials is a smart move for 2026 (A Different Market Is Coming In 2026: Sector Winners And Losers). I have also written that Berkshire could be an extremely interesting pick to have exposure to hidden assets in American manufacturing (Berkshire: One Beautiful Way To Play The Long XLI/XLU Thesis In 2026).

I am long on United Rentals (URI) because it combines exposure to construction and manufacturing activity with minimum exposure to residential activity. But companies such as Caterpillar (CAT) or government contractors are clear beneficiaries of this muted trend.

There is also another category of winners: payment processing companies such as Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA). While they don't directly win government contracts, government spending does add fuel to the economy. When Washington doesn't tighten its belt, transfer payments such as Social Security and Medicare keep flowing. Government benefits can be distributed via prepaid cards. When government-driven income increases, the overall spending rate in the economy is expected to move up. This means more swipes. We could say that these companies are among the silent winners of the spending inertia, something really hard to stop.

3. Buybacks Won't Decelerate

Many companies are sitting on rich cash piles, which always catch the eyes of both managers and investors. The issue becomes how to deploy this cash at a decent ROI. There might be new projects, but not always do mature companies find them interesting. M&A is always an option, but it has to make sense, and it has to go through regulatory scrutiny. In a thriving economy, companies simply love buybacks. They are an easy and cheap way to return capital to the shareholders while boosting EPS, which usually makes the top management team earn hefty bonuses. While S&P Global shows that buybacks are indeed more volatile than dividends, they are, generally speaking, moving up. They might be reduced when companies face important headwinds, but as soon as the tide turns, they are quickly resumed. While I am not a fan of companies racing to buy back their own shares when the stock price is too high (I love companies that buy back shares during recessions), I do understand the dynamics and the mechanics behind this trend (I actually wrote my bachelor's thesis on this topic).

According to S&P Global, buybacks are also a top-heavy phenomenon, with 49.5% of Q3 2025 buybacks concentrated in the top 20 companies of the market. In Q3 2025, we also saw for the second time in history the TTM period buybacks surpassing the $1T landmark, which represents a 1.79% yield. Overall, the TTM expenditure at the end of September was up 11.1% YoY, confirming the upward trajectory. The top two sectors are Information Technology and Financials.

With the S&P 500 earnings expected to grow between 12% and 15% this year (2026: The Revenge Of The S&P 493 [Market Outlook]), the support for big share reduction strategies is more than intact.

Here, companies such as Apple and Alphabet surely stand out. But I would truly consider the top banks. We will have time this year to talk about this, but I think that financial institutions will keep making a lot of money both because, even in the case of a dovish FED, interest rates won't go to zero, and because AI could truly simplify their internal operations and reduce their headcount.

4. The Labor Market Won't Crack

Yes, we hear about the K-shaped economy. We know the labor market is cooling. I think we will hardly see millions of idle workers sitting on the sidelines. There is a structural tightness in the U.S. labor market that won't go away in 2026. Let me explain why. First of all, it is about demographics. The working population is aging, while workers over 65 are increasing, with the replacement rate not high enough to compensate for retirees. We see that labor force growth has been slowing down across the most recent business cycles, and it is especially low for U.S.-born workers.

Therefore, as boomers retire, Gen Z isn't able to fill the new job vacancies at a fast enough pace. This means that the labor supply keeps being scarce. While this also has some implications for the national GDP and the way immigration policy is handled. Sometimes, we hear people talking about "The Silver Tsunami". Demographics are really hard to counterbalance. The lack of new workers will keep the unemployment rate from moving up to recessionary levels. Moreover, wage stickiness will remain present, as skilled workers will find themselves with increasingly high pricing power. Now, when job growth slows, the overall real GDP suffers, and countermeasures are deployed. The latest report from Indeed Hiring Lab expects job opening stabilization in 2026, with a tight labor supply in areas such as construction, medicine, and engineering. This means that unemployment could easily remain below 5%, if not even below 4.5%, while job openings will remain high because skilled workers are hard to find.

We also know that the participation rate in the job market is 62.5% (as of November 2025), according to the BLS. This might make us think that there is ample room to find new workers if job offerings are interesting enough (both in terms of salary, flexibility, or other benefits that would motivate people staying at home to go back to work). However, the prime-age labor force (those whose age ranges from 24 to 54) is actually almost fully employed, with a participation rate of 89% for men and close to 78% for women. In particular, the women's labor force participation rate (LFPR) is increasing more rapidly than for men, meaning that labor supply is truly going to be scarce.

Therefore, companies that might navigate through some troubled waters will in any case seriously ponder whether to enact layoffs or not, knowing that once they recover, it will be truly hard to fill the new vacancies.

In this environment, what companies should we favor? Well, those that are not handcuffed by labor immediately stand out. The first example is Amazon. While the company does employ a lot of people, it is at the forefront of automation and robotics, and it is rapidly replacing repetitive tasks in its warehouses with robots specifically built for this purpose. But I also see favorable software providers, where AI can improve productivity while reducing headcount. This is why I have recently become bullish on Adobe (ADBE). But I would also consider companies that provide autonomous solutions. One is, undoubtedly, Alphabet with Waymo (Alphabet Has Won). Another is John Deere (DE), with its autonomous tractors. Caterpillar is also part of this group. But we could explore companies such as Fanuc (FANUY) or Rockwell Automation (ROK).

5. Cheap Debt Won't Roll Over

This year, the bill comes due. Cheap corporate debt is expiring, and companies won't be able to postpone their refinancing needs. When interest rates started rising, many believed it would be a short-lived curve that would eventually bring us back to a zero-interest-rate environment. By now, it is clear it won't be so for some time. The exceptional financing conditions seen between the GFC and 2021 are exactly this: exceptional, meaning that they stand out uniquely from the average. There are billions in bonds and loans that are maturing between 2026 and 2028 (in 2028 alone, $3T of corporate debt will mature), with companies that might end up paying 2x or 3x the interest they paid until now. S&P Gòlobal expects that this year alone, $1.35T of nonfinancial corporate debt will mature, a 10% increase YoY, most of which was issued in 2020 and 2021, when interest rates were extremely low. This means that the expected funding cost will increase by at least 150 bps compared to prior rates.

Even if this year the Fed will become more dovish with Jerome Powell's successor, I still expect to see interest rates around 3% (perhaps 2.75%), more or less neutral versus inflation. It would be a very bold and dangerous move for the dollar to reduce interest rates below this threshold, unless the FED forgets about its dual mandate and favors the return of hot inflation. In this environment, highly leveraged companies will suffer more than others.

This brings us to favor companies with solid balance sheets that have low net debt or high earnings power (the ratio between pre-tax earnings and interest expenses). But a world without debt is almost impossible to conceive. So, the real winners will be debt rating agencies, such as S&P Global (SPGI) and Moody's (MCO). I own S&P Global and bought more last year as the stock retraced back in August. But I think Moody's is just another fine option.

There will be victims, especially among zombie companies.

And guess who is perfectly equipped to weather this situation? Berkshire Hathaway, thanks to its immense cash and Treasury position, which puts it at the crossroads of many refinancing strategies.

Conclusion

I wanted to talk about these five "non-events" because they actually show an important reality. Investors seek predictability, and often the best ally is the absence of changes instead of forecasting what the new trends will be. Recognizing what won't happen in 2026 could be as valuable, if not even more valuable, than understanding what will change and what new trends will spring forth. Without a doubt, this year, as always, we will hear a lot of noise, daily headlines will make our investment decisions waver, we will see policy pivots, sudden geopolitical shifts, unexpected events, and so on. However, being aware of what will remain the same could be the best way to anchor our investments to constancy and predictability, which is something that helps the magic of compounding happen. Owning the "Non-Event Kings" is the first and smartest choice we can make to strengthen our portfolio and be able to sail across unpredictable waters.

Some might say that this is a mindset somewhat akin to what we said about Charlie Munger at the beginning of this article: the best investments allow an investor to sit idle without doing anything, valuing inactivity as much as good stock picking. In this case, we could say that our opportunity cost is to give up being surprised by unexpected events while being comfortably invested in obvious picks that are often underdiscussed due to noisy financial narratives.

data.title: Olaplex: Buying The Takeover News
data.description:
With the news that Olaplex (OLPX) has received a takeover offer and the subsequent 20% jump in its stock price on January 7th, how does an investor or potential investor view the prospects of the stock? Has the move already been made, or is there still room to run? Acquisitions can be binary results for investors, as stock prices may rocket higher when a deal is announced but collapse just as quickly if an offer doesn’t come to fruition. Consequently, a savvy investor has to be sure that the company in question is sound and worthy of investment absent the acquisition. In my opinion, I believe Olaplex has stabilized and can be a good investment at the current price with or without a deal. At these levels, there’s still room for significant upside potential, but the downside is underpinned by the company’s positive cash flows and momentum established by the new management team.

New Management Team in Place

I have been impressed with the new management team that was put in place over the past two years. It’s not an easy task to turn around a company that was very clearly struggling, as quarterly revenues were cut in half from their highs of mid-2022, but that was the mission for new CEO Amanda Baldwin and team after she took over in early 2024. This was the cliché falling knife, and no one had any idea how far the fall was going to be. Looking at the last 15 quarters of sales, it does appear that some stabilization has finally occurred:

More than that, though, is the way it has occurred. There’s no magic formula or lever to pull, so Baldwin began with a top-down approach by launching a strategic plan in early 2025, “Bonds and Beyond,” after spending the first year understanding the company and her customers. Many will see this strategic plan as a very “corporate” move and one that is not all that substantive, and I do agree that companies have very mixed track records with programs such as these. One only needs to look in the same industry at Estee Lauder’s disastrous “Profit Recovery and Growth Plan” as an example of what not to do. With that piece of skepticism in mind, I do think it is important to get the entire company moving in one direction. But it is not just about vision; it is also about tactics and execution. And that is where I see a glimmer of hope. An example of this was the launch of two hair mask products in late August that have outperformed expectations, according to CEO Amanda Baldwin in the Q3 2025 conference call. Olaplex’s foundation is based on scientific innovation, not just producing more of the same product for a consumer to replace every three to six months. This product launch’s 360 degree marketing effort was well coordinated with professionals, beauty influencers, pop-up stores, and a dizzying PR campaign that appears to have been effective at creating a buzz for the new products.

Creating demand is the first tenet of the new strategic vision, and so far it appears that they have managed to do so, exceeding Q3 revenues and earnings expectations. Absent any acquisition news, I believe that Q4 earnings and, more importantly, 2026 guidance are major milestones and a window into whether Olaplex will continue to stumble, or finally break through and achieve the momentum that was lost a few years back with the negative publicity associated with the hair loss lawsuit that was eventually dismissed.

A Potential Suitor

News broke Wednesday afternoon that Henkel (HENKY) had submitted a takeover offer to acquire Olaplex, with a deal potentially coming in the next few weeks.

Henkel is a EUR 21 billion German company that has been around for 150 years and has acquired many consumer brands in many different industries. One of those industries is personal care, and that specifically includes 15 hair care brands, with the largest being Schwarzkopf. Olaplex seems to be a natural fit with their current brands, and financially, a deal would not be that taxing based on the billions of cash currently on Henkel's balance sheet. Could the news leaking impact the amount Henkel is willing to pay? That’s certainly a possibility given the recent stock price spike, but I can’t imagine the Olaplex Board would be entertaining an acquisition price that could be thwarted by a 20% move in the stock. It might result in a lower premium on the actual announcement date, but it is likely to be far higher than 20% of the trading price of Olaplex before this news broke.

Valuation and Potential Premium

What type of premium would Olaplex require in order to actually sell the company? Normally, one would assume that at a minimum 30-40% is an acceptable deal premium, but is that really the case for a company that dropped in price from $22 to the current price of approximately $1.65, having dropped even lower only a few months ago? Would the board accept an acquisition price of, say, $2.30 (which is just shy of 40% above $1.65), or would this not be in the long-term interests of shareholders? How would the 20% jump on Wednesday impact all of these discussions?

But before we just assume that a deal is going to get done, let’s look at Olaplex’s valuation as a standalone entity. As an investor, you HAVE to be comfortable with the company as it currently stands because an acquisition is uncertain at best, even with the recent news reported. As I previously wrote, Olaplex’s earnings are hampered by substantial intangible amortization, which makes looking at a price to earnings ratio very difficult. I generally prefer enterprise value to free cash flow anyway, but especially in cases such as this, given the intangible amortization. Currently, EV/FCF is approximately 18.5x based on a stock price of $1.65. That’s a little on the high side, but not terrible in this market for a company of this size. When you add the possibility of an acquisition, I wouldn’t hesitate to pay this amount right now.

Risks

So what happens if a deal is not consummated? Is an investor left with a company that is down 93% from its IPO and struggling to make money? Well, as mentioned above, I do believe that management is turning the business around, and Q4 earnings and the 2026 outlook are major touchpoints in determining how successful that turnaround is going. If a deal doesn’t happen, the stock is most likely going to give back Wednesday’s gains, but I don’t think all is lost for Olaplex investors. The company continues to have positive earnings (though just barely) and positive free cash flow that it can use to reinvigorate its business through continued marketing investments.

I think the biggest risk of all is if the company doesn’t get acquired and Q4 and/or the 2026 outlook is weak. At that point one must question whether the strategic plan is taking hold at all. While the signs and momentum appear to be there, the Company must execute in the short term to reinforce that they are on the right track.

Conclusion

A rumored acquisition is not necessarily a reason to buy a stock, but I do think that there are other positives to Olaplex’s business that can mitigate the downside should the deal not come to fruition. With the new management team in place for well over a year now and coming off a stronger than expected third quarter, Olaplex is in a good position to turn around its business and build momentum into 2026. A potential acquisition offers a strong upside for investors and speculators alike.

data.title: Retire With A Potential $5,000 Monthly Income And High Growth
data.description:
Introduction:

It is that time of the year when a lot of people make new resolutions only to forget them in a few weeks or sometimes a couple of months. To plan for retirement, there is no better time than today, especially if you have crossed the 50-year mark. However, someone famous once said, "Plans are useless; planning is essential". So, unless a plan is backed by proper planning, it may prove to be futile. You may have many plans for retirement, such as pursuing your hobbies, touring the world, or downsizing and settling down in a retirement-friendly place. But all said and done, they may remain distant goals unless you prepare a blueprint for how you are going to achieve them. In fact, the planning process basically involves defining your goals and sub-goals and preparing a blueprint of your detailed action plan. Also needed is a process to measure your progress and success rate on a periodic basis. If the progress is not satisfactory, the action plan may need to be modified/adjusted.

Retirement Planning:

First, you need to define clear-cut goals, action plans, and a process for how to implement them. Preferably, consider defining the best-case and the worst-case scenarios. A realistic scenario will fall somewhere between the two. Also, divide your goals into smaller periodic goals to be able to measure the success rate. For example, if you are planning to save $500,000 (excluding growth) over the next 20 years, divide this over every 5-year period as well as on an annual basis. When it comes to growth, it may be difficult to define annual goals, as outcomes would depend greatly on the market environment, but 5-year goals may still be useful.

You may like to start with the following:

  • The amount of capital you need to accumulate prior to retirement to meet your retirement objectives.
  • The time horizon for achieving retirement goals.
  • The fallback or alternative options in case of a shortfall.

The amount of retirement capital that is needed would vary greatly from person to person. It all depends on your own expenses and the acceptable living standard that you are accustomed to. Obviously, the time factor (the number of years between now and the age at which you would like to retire) is important. The more time you have, the easier the path will be. That's why most financial planners would emphasize starting early. Also, flexibility is important, as there are many factors that are outside your control, such as the market and economic environment, as well as unforeseeable events in the future. For example, if your goal was to retire at age 65, you should be prepared to retire early as well as be ready to work a bit longer.

Once the primary and critical factors and goals have been defined, it may be time to write down secondary goals and financial parameters. For example, what is the minimum (or maximum) income that you should be aiming to withdraw from your portfolio? Another example would be to define your tolerance for drawdowns and corrections. This one is tough to define, as most folks overestimate their capacity to tolerate drawdowns without panicking. Then there are smaller issues, such as whether to invest in funds or individual stocks.

Below, we will lay out a planning process and an investment portfolio for a hypothetical investor; however, we will try to make it somewhat generalized and flexible so that it would suit a large number of folks with their own customization.

The portfolio presented should suit investors at any age, but this would be especially useful for folks who are 10 to 15 (preferably 15) years away from retirement.

So, let's define our financial goals:

  • 5% to 6% income level
  • Overall, 10% or better growth of the capital
  • Safety of the capital- drawdowns to be 70-80% of the S&P500
  • Minimal maintenance effort.

Income may not be important initially, but gradually, as you approach retirement, the importance of income takes center stage. So, we will form a portfolio that is appropriate for both stages, pre-retirement and post-retirement. Moreover, over the long term (10-15 years), we should expect our portfolio to provide the necessary growth that matches the long-term growth of the stock market. In addition, we want our portfolio to be less volatile and have lower drawdowns than the broader market. This is a very desirable feature, especially for conservative investors. Lastly, but not least, we want the portfolio to require minimal effort to maintain.

Portfolio Structure:

We will have three components (or buckets) in this portfolio:

  • A Buy-and-Hold Funds-based DGI bucket: – 50% allocation
  • A Hedging/Rotation Bucket—30% Allocation.
  • A Buy-and-Hold Asset-Diversified Bucket—20% Allocation

Though we have specified the allocations to each bucket, this is highly flexible, and each investor should adjust the allocations based on their income needs, risk tolerance, and personal preferences. Each bucket has its specific function and purpose (discussed below) in the overall portfolio.

The second bucket is a "Hedging" or "Rotational" bucket. Basically, the goal is to provide an in-built safety (partial) mechanism against deep drawdowns and keep the overall volatility lower than the broader market. If the broader market drops by 30%, we expect the rotational bucket to fall only by half or two-thirds; however, there are no guarantees of future circumstances. The downside of the rotational bucket is that it will require some effort (and trading) on a monthly basis. Due to monthly trading, it works best inside a tax-deferred account. We expect this bucket to provide between 4%-6% income and market-matching or exceeding growth.

The third bucket (with 20% allocation) is of the buy-and-hold type. It will be invested in 5 securities that represent different types of asset classes. The idea is that they are likely to have some variance in their behavior under different economic environments.

The overall structure of the portfolio provides a lot of flexibility in terms of how many buckets we may want to have or what allocations we want to assign to each bucket. If someone wants to have a hands-off approach, they can simply have the two buy-and-hold buckets and avoid the rotational bucket. These buckets are the building blocks of the framework that you can use to form your own portfolio to suit your individual needs.

(I) A Funds-Based DGI and Income Bucket: 50% Allocation

This bucket is the core bucket of this portfolio, and that's why we are suggesting allocating half of the total capital. This bucket will also produce a reasonably high income, with an average of 6% yield.

We recently published an article about a buy-and-hold fund-based portfolio with 10 funds. Here is the link to the article. We will use the same (or similar) portfolio for this bucket. Here is the portfolio with the current income yield. You could read more details on individual funds in the above article.

The securities are (SCHD), (ADX), (GPIQ), (BST), (RNP), (GLDM), (VAW), (PEO), (AMLP), and (UTG).

Table-1:

(II) The Hedging (ROTATIONAL) Bucket: (30% Allocation)

With 50% of the portfolio allocated to a DGI-oriented fund-based bucket, we will now introduce a bucket that will provide hedging and reduce volatility and drawdowns and provide some stability during times of panic and deep corrections. We will allocate 30% of the total funds to this bucket.

For this monthly rotational bucket, we will choose FIVE core securities, one each from a different asset class with a low correlation with each other. However, we will select only two securities every month for investment for the following month. There will also be a hedging security that would replace the core securities if some conditions are met (explained below).

Table-2: The list of securities:

As we can see, three out of the five securities are closed-end funds, and they generally provide high income. However, there is a fifth security (10-year mid-term Treasury) that acts as a fallback or hedging security.

Rotation Method: Every month, we will calculate the total performance returns (including any distributions) over the previous three months and seven months for each of the five securities. We will assign 50% weightage to each of the two periods (3-month and 7-month performance) to calculate the average performance. We will select the two top securities that have performed the highest in terms of total returns (including any dividends/distributions). If the performance of any of the two selected securities is lower than the risk-free return provided by cash (or 1-3 month Treasury), then it is replaced with the IEF-Treasury fund for the next month; otherwise, it is retained. This process is repeated at the end of each month.

Some folks may find this rotation method somewhat complex, and that is certainly a negative. However, it can provide high growth, moderate monthly income, usually 3-4%, and much lower drawdowns than the S&P 500.

With 50% and 30% allocated to DGI and Rotation strategies, we will now allocate 20% to another buy-and-hold bucket. This bucket will provide the simplicity of buy-and-hold but provide high growth, moderate income, and diversity of many different asset classes. The idea behind this bucket is that these securities will have significant variance among themselves as they come from many different asset classes.

Please note that this portfolio may be as volatile (or sometimes more) as the S&P 500. But based on past performance, it is likely to outperform the S&P 500. The securities are the same as the rotation bucket, but they are held on a buy-and-hold basis and in equal proportion.

The list of securities:

  • ProShares Ultra QQQ ETF (QLD) [or Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ)]

The Combined Portfolio:

Now, let's see how the overall portfolio would behave. Though the future will always be somewhat different than the past, backtesting can provide us with some general idea. We already have the test results from buckets 2 and 3. To test bucket 1 for 15 years or more, we will need to make some replacements. For example, we will allocate the share of SCHD to ADX, AMLP to Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and replace GPIQ and BST with some other similar fund, such as Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund II (EOS).

Here is the comparative chart for the growth of $100,000 put in the combined model portfolio versus the S&P 500. As per the backtesting results, the combined portfolio would have provided an annualized growth rate of 14% since 2008, compared to 10.9% for the S&P 500.
