Risks:

There are always risks with any stock investments. The portfolio described above has a high concentration in equity investments. Though there are some bonds and fixed-income investments, the equity investments take the majority share. The portfolio employs a hedging strategy in the form of a rotational bucket; however, the allocation is at best 1/3 of the overall portfolio, and even so, any hedging mechanism has limitations. Also, it may be worth pointing out that there may be some level of duplication of some stocks between the first two buckets; however, we think it should be minor. Further, no stock portfolio can be totally immune to deep corrections or impacts from a recession. We believe the income from the portfolio should help in such situations, but income may also fall to some extent in a prolonged recession.

Concluding Thoughts:

In our view, the model portfolio presented in this article is highly diversified, provides reasonable income for most folks, and is simple enough to implement.

The combined model portfolio will provide roughly 5.5% to 6% income, or roughly $60,000 annually ($5,000 monthly) on a $1 million portfolio. We think one should not aim to withdraw more than 6%, as that would impact the long-term growth of the portfolio. Also, an income-based portfolio makes the ride much smoother, as there would be no need to sell shares during a market correction or at the bottom.

That being said, many readers may find the rotational bucket a bit complex. If that is the case, you could forgo the rotation bucket and look for some other hedging alternatives. One such example would be the JPMorgan Hedged Equity Laddered ETF (HELO). But the fund has 0.50% expenses, provides a very low-income yield, and overall growth may be inferior to the rotation method.

data.title: Alphabet's 'Ironwood' Singularity And The Inevitability Of The Zero-Marginal-Cost Compute Flywheel
data.description:
Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) stock is a strong buy for me. Alphabet has a highly positive risk-reward profile among the Magnificent 7. I am saying this not just because of Google’s search monopoly, but because Alphabet can be the largest vertically integrated AI company. It is different from Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META) that remain beholden to the ‘Nvidia (NVDA) Tax’ on compute. Alphabet has closed the loop on the AI value chain. Alphabet now owns the energy (nuclear/geothermal deals), the silicon (7th Gen TPUs/Ironwood), the infrastructure (Google Cloud/Data Centers), the model (Gemini), and the distribution (Android/Chrome/YouTube).

So, in my opinion, Wall Street is currently mispricing Alphabet as a legacy ad utility facing disruption and not pricing in its transition into a hardware-software hybrid AI-comp capable of compressing inference costs faster than others. The primary risk is not technological obsolescence here, and it is also not the regulatory fracture, as it cannot sever the distribution arm/line for Gemini. However, the real risk is the Agentic disintermediation of ads. Hence, GOOG’s current pricing offers a free option on Waymo’s autonomous market lead and the margin expansion from custom silicon. The update from BofA on the daily active users data on leading models moves me to assess Alphabet stock upside and risks.

GOOG Stock Forward Technicals Indicate A ~90% Price Upside In FY2026

GOOG’s weekly price chart holds the Fibonacci Golden Spiral breakout on a multi-year basis. The pattern signals that GOOG is exiting a multi-week consolidation phase and entering an impulse wave (Elliott Wave). The current price is now above a critical resistance (now support) point that is the upper bound of the Trend Pivot’s Range of Influence. A stable uptrend along with a diagonal trendline (grey) will clearly invalidate bearish theses and initiate a high-pace move toward $427 (35% price upside) near the 2.118 Fibonacci extension. However, there can be an extended consolidation over/around $295, and the momentum indicators mark this possibility. The MACD (7.59) is crossing down into bearish as the histogram is weakening on the longer-bullish-mode and may enter a shorter-bearish mode.

A consolidation can accelerate buying pressure, as a stabilizing RSI (77.70) over 70 is pointing to solid bullish strength instead of exhaustion. It is a mark of super-cycle moves where the stock remains overbought for extended durations (during price discovery). Side-by-side, the stochastic oscillator at 91.73 has stabilized for more than 2 quarters and confirms this embedded high bullish momentum. Now, why am I saying the longer-bullish-mode and shorter-bearish-mode (super-cycle), because RSI and Stochastic oscillator have dropped quickly towards oversold states (30 and 20) (see Q3-2024 and Q1-2025) and then resumed trend reversion constructively/slowly towards overbought levels over the mid-term.

The main point here is that there is a terminal velocity target of $599 (90% price upside) based on the golden ratio (near 3.618 extension) for FY2026. The diagonal trend initiated in early Q2 suggests that GOOG stock price is technically running up under the realization of AI monetization over 6-week (dark yellow) and 13-week (red) EMAs. The main support floor has shifted higher to the $295 level (1.0 extension), so any pullbacks toward this level, the EMAs ($296-$313 range), and trend-pivot (at $234) can provide a highly favorable risk/reward accumulation zone to average down the long position after the main buy at the current price.

The Zero-Marginal-Cost Compute Flywheel

The unit-economics decoupling through TPU maturation (the Ironwood 7th Gen architecture) is the long-term bullish catalyst for GOOG/GOOGL stock price. Although Wall Street is tilted on earnings beat on revenue and EPS, in my opinion, the lever for Alphabet’s forward valuation expansion is the collapse of its Internal Cost of Compute [ICC] relative to Microsoft (OPENAI) and Meta as they engaged in a CapEx race. A heavy percentage of Microsoft’s, OpenAI’s, and Meta’s every dollar of CapEx spent flows directly to Nvidia/AMD (AMD), capping their long-term gross margins on AI services and at consolidated levels. Google’s 7th Gen TPU (Ironwood) delivers a 10x peak-performance improvement over TPU v5p and 4x better performance per chip. This is a deflationary tool as AI models shift from training (capital-intensive) to inference (OpEx), the cost to serve a query becomes the determinant of profitability.

For more clarity, in my opinion, Google’s ability to run Gemini on internal silicon can drive the marginal cost of an AI search query toward zero far faster than Bing and ChatGPT, which are shackled to commercial GPU pricing and margins. This silicon advantage creates a Compute Flywheel. First, it leads to lower inference costs, as Google can afford to infuse heavy AI models (like Gemini 3 Deep Think) into free products (AI Studio, Search, YouTube, Gmail) without destroying margins. Direct competitors may not match this without incurring losses/charging relatively higher subscription fees, in my opinion. The second thing is Data Gravity. Lower costs allow Google to process more tokens (already processing 1.3 quadrillion monthly tokens, +20x YoY) than the competitors. This massive data ingestion goes back into model optimization. As a result, it is creating a data-based moat that improves the TPUs' efficiency on a forward basis. The last factor is external monetization (Anthropic is using 1 million TPUs). Google is now monetizing its silicon advantage by renting it to competitors and rebuilding a cost center (infrastructure) into a high-margin revenue that hedges against Nvidia’s market grip.

As per Bank of America (BAC) report, Gemini’s daily active user growth (+17% MoM) is larger than ChatGPT’s (+2% MoM). It is indicating that the growth momentum for OpenAI is flattening, and Google’s AI integration is getting higher-paced. The addition of 9 million daily active users for Gemini versus only 6 million for ChatGPT is an clear indication that Google’s Interface Layer is holding on to its 2.7 billion daily search visits and leading to a 351% YoY hyper-surge in Gemini usage. This confirms that Google’s AI products are functioning as an additive engagement layer. It is now a super-app extension that is creating a dual-moat structure as Google retains the high-margin query volume and captures the high-complexity compute volume.

Furthermore, this trend supports my Inference Cost Arbitrage argument. By shifting 62 million daily active users onto internal 7th Generation TPUs, Google is actively driving its marginal cost of inference toward zero, while competitors like OpenAI (whose traffic is stalling) and Bing (whose traffic is declining 9%). The divergence in traffic (the 24% MoM rise in Gemini visits against the contraction in the other platforms) suggests that Google’s aggressive deployment of AI Overviews and progressive training of user behavior to prefer multimodal (integrated answers over standalone chatbots) thereby re-closing the loop on the digital advertising monopoly before competitors can reach a critical mass.

Moreover, this hardware advantage extends to energy. The Project Suncatcher initiative and nuclear deals support Google to decouple from the commercial energy grid. By vertically integrating energy > silicon > model > application, Google constructs a forward margin profile that Wall Street models (that simply extrapolate current CapEx trends) are failing to project. When Wall Street completely realizes that Google’s AI CapEx is actually purchasing deflationary assets (chips/TPUs that lower forward OpEx) rather than inflationary assets (chips/GPUs that depreciate rapidly), GOOG/GOOGL stock will re-rate from an ad/media utility multiple to a hard-tech infrastructure multiple, in my opinion.

If I apply AI-adjusted PE multiple and EPS as the TPU margin advantage materializes, GOOG stock can be valued towards the high-end of P/E multiples (forward and TTM) expansion to 29x-32x by 2026’s end. The use of the high-end consensus FY2026 EPS of $13.11 (considering the EPS revision trend) with 13% mid-term average surprise-% (quarterly, since the Q1-2023 AI phase) provides a 2026 GOOG target price range of $429-$474 in line with the $427 Fibonacci target. However, on an FY2027 basis, with EPS growing to ~$14.13 (high end, based on buybacks and margin expansion), that range can be $463-$511 interim to $599 Fibonacci target. I am not using the DCF model here, as it heavily penalizes Alphabet stock for its $90B+ CapEx spend (treating it as a drag on FCF). With improving ROIC, ROA, and ROE, the valuation penalty/cap will not be there.

The Agentic Disintermediation of Ads

For GOOG/GOOGL stock price, antitrust regulation is not a major forward negative (as it takes years and often results in impotent fines). It is the structural collapse of the Ten Blue Links business model due to Agentic AI. The logic behind disintermediation is that Google's trillion-dollar business is built on a specific user behavior [query to list of links to user selection to click (ad revenue)], however, the shift to Agentic AI (through Project Jarvis and Gemini) breaks this chain. I am trying to say that if a user asks Gemini (or OpenAI, or Siri-Apple Intelligence) to ‘Book a flight to London under $600’ and the AI agent can execute directly via an API. Hence, the user will not see a search results page and so they will also not see a sponsored link/display ad. As a result, the impression volume (main metric of the Google Network and YouTube Ads) can theoretically collapse even if query volume increases.

Why is this an important risk? Because AI Overviews increase engagement, but they gloss over the monetization density of these overviews. As of now, Google monetizes intent. However, Agentic AI fulfills intent without the intermediate step of shopping for a solution. If Google shifts from a high-margin auction model (selling clicks) to a lower-margin transaction model (taking a % of the booking), profitability per query can drop. Along with that, Alphabet faces the innovator's dilemma. To win in AI, it must deploy agents that actively reduce the surface area for core ad products. If it doesn't, OpenAI and others will. To support this, what I see as problematic is the decline in Google Network revenue (-2.6% YoY, in Q3). It indicates that third-party web properties (where Google serves ads) are already losing traffic to zero-click AI answers. Logically, if this trend accelerates and bleeds into core Search, the valuation-multiple compression can be severe. If quarterly earnings in 2026 show that AI-Mode queries are monetizing at lower rates than traditional search queries, GOOG stock can retest the $234 and $173 levels.

Alphabet Pre-Q4 And Post-Q4 Earnings Alpha Protocol

Pre-Q4 Earnings [The Ironwood Accumulation Phase (Current to 3 February 2026)]

In the immediate weeks leading up to the Q4 earnings, bulls can aggressively position for a volatility compression squeeze above $295. The market is in a price discovery mode for the efficacy of Google’s TPU Ironwood. The move here is to front-run the possible analyst revisions that will occur once the Ironwood efficiency gains are registered on the Q4 call. Alphabet expects 2025 CapEx to land between $91 billion-$93 billion. The market can interpret this as a cost burden. However, my thesis marks this as a deflationary asset acquisition. Hence, any sudden price weakness based on AI Bubble-FOMO means to buy more shares as near to $295 support as possible. The market is not focused on the margin accretion/leap that results from shifting workloads from Nvidia H100s (high OpEx/CapEx) to internal Ironwood TPUs (lower OpEx/CapEx). The period prior to Q4 is about betting on the industrialization of inference.

The Earnings Event: The Capex Shock Paradox

When Q4 results are announced, the numbers (revenue/EPS) can be secondary to the 2026 CapEx outlook. Alphabet already signaled (in the Q3-2025 call) a big increase in 2026 CapEx. An algorithmic sell-off is highly probable if the 2026 CapEx outlook exceeds decisively without a side-by-side jump in Cloud revenue. This volatility event represents the entry point within the Trend Pivot's Range of Influence. The major metric to watch during Q4 earnings is the Cloud Operating Margin. In Q3, Cloud operating margin expanded to ~24% (up from 17% of Q3-2024). If Alphabet guides for huge 2026 CapEx but projects stable/expanding Cloud margins, it proves (mathematically) that internal silicon (TPUs) is decoupling the cost structure from the rest of the industry. Therefore, interpret a Capex Shock with margin stability as the main buy signal.

Post-Q4 Earnings [The Waymo And Agentic Realization (February 2026 - Mid 2026)]

Following the Q4 call, the market’s view may shift from infrastructure costs to asset realization. The post-earnings bulls must focus on valuing the hidden assets that are approaching green escape, like Waymo’s expansion into Tokyo, London, Miami, Dallas, Houston, and San Antonio, alongside 1 million rides/month. Post-Q4, bulls must monitor for a spinoff signal/a separate disclosure for Waymo that can instantly realize more than $100 billion in enterprise value trapped in the Other Bets valuation drag. Moreover, as Agentic AI (Gemini acting on behalf of users) moves from testing to deployment, GOOG/GOOGL stock will re-rate based on Service Revenue rather than Ad Revenue. If post-earnings data ticks that AI-Mode queries are monetizing through transaction fees (like booking a flight directly) at a rate comparable to traditional Search ads, the risk of disintermediation can become inactive.

_

Overall, my stance on GOOG/GOOGL is Aggressive Long and Accumulate More on Dips. The merging of the technical breakout structure with the inflection of the Ironwood TPU cycle creates a big opportunity with forward monitorables: a stable breakout above the swing high of $330 (to confirm the impulse phase toward $427), Q4 operating margin in Google Cloud [if this crosses 25% (currently ~24%), it can confirm that the TPU cost advantage is real], and Other Bets losses (any reduction here through Waymo commercialization is pure profit upside).

data.title: Love Direct Deposit? You'll Love Automatic Data Processing
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

How often do you get paid?

For most, they get paid every two weeks. However, some of us get paid on a monthly basis or even a weekly basis. Some companies offer you the option to cash out at the end of every shift if you want! We've grown to love and expect that deposit landing in our bank account exactly when the company promises it will.

It wasn't that long ago that paychecks were physical checks that you would have to run down to your local bank branch to deposit. I can remember that my dad picked his bank account because the branch was just down the street from where he worked. He would get his paycheck every two weeks, drive to the bank, and deposit it on his way home. Now, though, the vast majority of us receive that deposit electronically. Those electronic deposits are processed primarily through some form of payroll software.

Part of my mission in building a dividend-paying portfolio is getting paid by everyday activities that Americans, Canadians, and Europeans enjoy regularly. One thing that I believe we all enjoy is getting paid.

So the question then becomes, how do you get paid for other people getting paid? Today, we're going to answer that question.

Let's dive in!

This Dividend King is on Sale Now

Automatic Data Processing (ADP) is the world's largest human capital management software company. Across its 1.1 million clients and counting, ADP is responsible for paying more than 42 million employees around the globe.

What is the appeal of ADP? The company's software and services are essential to operate in the business world today. Any company with employees must do payroll, keep track of time and attendance, and administer benefits. Few companies have the time or desire to do these things in-house, so many let the likes of ADP handle them.

Looking ahead, the company believes that it can generate 6% to 7% annual revenue growth over the medium term. ADP's path to doing so includes expanding its global footprint and adapting to changing client needs to maintain its business bookings growth momentum. What's more, the company is focusing on enhancing its ADP Assist platform to drive client engagement. In the last year, ADP Assist has helped with over 5.5 million client conversations, according to President and CEO Maria Black's opening remarks during the Q1 2026 Earnings Call.

Strategic investments aimed at improving operating efficiency should further bolster ADP's profit margins. Along with modest share repurchases (1% annual share count reductions), this is how the company expects to generate 9% to 11% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth in the coming years.

That jives with the FAST Graphs analyst consensus of 9.3% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth through FY 2028, off a FY 2025 base of $10.01.

Financially, ADP is a fortress. The company maintains an AA- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

ADP's 2.7% dividend yield is reasonably attractive. This is a great yield on cost play in my opinion. That's because ADP has grown its dividend by 11.5% annually over the last five years. This equates to a Chowder Number of around 14%. The payout ratio is set to be in the high-50% range, which should allow ADP to lengthen its half-century dividend growth streak.

In my view, ADP is a classic example of a great business trading at an enticing valuation. At the current $258 share price, it's priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 22.6 (52% weight for FY 2026 and a 48% weight for FY 2027).

For more color, ADP's 10-year average P/E ratio is 28.6. I think that this remains a sensible fair value multiple. That's because its 10% annual adjusted diluted EPS growth target (the midpoint of 9-11%) isn't far off its 10-year average CAGR of 13.2%. As Kody noted in his previous article on ADP, the company is more profitable now than it has been in years past. This also backs up my fair value per share estimate of $326, which points to a 21% discount to fair value.

If ADP lives up to expectations and reverts to fair value, that would result in a 31% upside through calendar year 2026. By the end of FY 2031, ADP could log 15% annual total returns.

The HCM software company still faces risks, though. As Kody indicated in his September article, noteworthy risks to ADP include the potential for a major cyber breach and a competitive industry landscape.

Any event that tarnishes its brand image among clients could materially hurt its fundamentals. That's because, in such a case, ADP could be hit by an exodus of clients and regulatory penalties/litigation.

The company also operates in a highly fragmented industry. If it can't keep up with ever-changing client preferences, it could lose market share to competitors. This could also diminish its growth story.

Conclusion

ADP is a fantastic business that engages in an activity that we all enjoy tremendously. If you look forward to payday, you look forward to ADP potentially providing services to the company you work for, which makes that essential function operate. Very few people pay attention to their direct deposit, outside its amount, until it's missing. If it goes missing, all eyes turn to the company you work for, and their eyes turn to the software they use.

When everything runs smoothly, no one minds paying for the payroll software to achieve the results that they're looking for. Happy employees are productive employees. Productive employees drive strong earnings for the company they work for. Using ADP allows companies to keep employees happy by ensuring their payroll deposits arrive on time. ADP makes investors happy by providing them with strong earnings growth and strong dividend growth.

This is yet another wonderful way you can invest like a Dividend King and enjoy income from every single aspect of the economy and daily life. No need to chase the latest craze or the biggest buzzwords in the market. Forget the confusion and find clarity.

data.title: Alpha Pro Tech: Reliable Assets, Minimal Liabilities
data.description:
Introduction

Alpha Pro Tech (APT) is an obscure company. However, as any seasoned value investor would know, seemingly unexciting companies often, paradoxically, present the investor with the most exciting opportunities. APT has the potential to be one of these opportunities. Indeed, when conducting a hyper-conservative liquidation valuation, it could be argued that the company is, or at least has the potential to be, cheap. But when digging into the balance sheet a little further, the investor might come to realize that the business is even cheaper than a valuation based on the book value of its assets might suggest. When also considered in the context of the favorable qualitative aspects of the business, the investor may be willing to forgo some cheapness and still acquire shares at a reasonable price.

Business Summary

Alpha Pro Tech was founded in 1983 and is headquartered in Ontario, Canada. Alpha Pro Tech Ltd. is the parent company of subsidiaries Alpha Pro Tech, Inc., which manufactures and sells "disposable protective apparel and infection control products for the cleanroom, industrial, pharmaceutical, medical, and dental markets", and Alpha Pro Tech Engineered Products, which manufactures building supply construction weatherization products. Resultingly, the company has two segments: Disposable Protective Apparel (DPA) and Building Supply. The below image shows the company's products under their respective segments.

In full year 2024, Building Supply constituted 62% of revenue, with Disposable Protective Apparel making up the rest. Almost all of the company's revenues are generated in the U.S., with less than 1% coming from international customers in 2024. Information on the company's manufacturing base can be seen below.

Manufacturing Location Product
Valdosta, Georgia (Leased) Building Supply products
India (joint venture, 41.66% ownership) Building Supply & Disposable Protective Apparel products
Nogales, Arizona (Leased) Disposable Protective Apparel
Asia, Mexico (3rd party contract manufacturer) Disposable Protective Apparel

Valuation and Thesis

APT has a very healthy balance sheet. The company has a book value of $6.08 according to Seeking Alpha, which consists entirely of tangible assets. APT's net current asset value (NCAV) is roughly $3.90 as of the latest 10-Q filing. Importantly, the company's current assets are made up of 34% cash/equivalents, 45% inventory, 14% receivables, and 7% prepaid expenses. Since the majority of APT's current assets are in inventory and receivables, we cannot blindly take NCAV at book value, as the book value of inventory and receivables may be more than its actual liquidation value, which is what the NCAV calculation represents.

Of course, we could take the old Benjamin Graham approach of net-net working capital (NNWC) to value this, and discount inventory and receivables at uniform rates of 75% and 50%, respectively. With these adjustments, APT's NNWC value sits at $2.50, which, realistically, might be too conservative in light of the company's long history of stable operations and consistent profitability. The company has also not traded this low since 2017, making a return to that price anytime soon unlikely, especially when considering the share buybacks and resultant share count reduction since then.

Also, APT's inventory likely has a low risk of obsolescence. APT's inventory is broken up between the Building Supply and Disposable Protective Apparel segments. As of the most recent quarterly results, APT reports that the Building Supply segment's inventory book value is $11.9 million, whereas the Disposable Protective Apparel's book value is about $11.6 million. This is a roughly 50/50 split.

As can be seen above, the Building Supply segment consists primarily of inventory related to house wraps and synthetic roof underlayment. Intuitively, it would make sense that the sales generated from the Building Supply segment would correlate with housing starts in the U.S. Let's see how this has panned out over the past five years.

Year U.S. Housing Starts Growth Building Supply Sales Growth
2020 7% 15.1%
2021 16% 20.6%
2022 -3% 0.1%
2023 -8.5% 9.4%
2024 -3.7% -11%
2025 (3Q YoY) -2.6% 5.4%

In most years, save 2024, APT's Building Supply sales growth seems to have outperformed that of the housing starts in the U.S. One reason for this may be the growing demand for renovation and maintenance that is required as existing houses age, which increases secular demand for APT's Building Supply products.

Furthermore, the products that make up APT's Building Supply inventory are largely non-perishable and not susceptible to erratically changing consumer tastes. This is further corroborated by the lack of substantial inventory write-downs that have occurred over the past decade or so, as well as stable gross margins over the same period. Indeed, even during the 2008 housing crash, there were no inventory write downs. Keeping this in mind, let's apply a conservative 80% discount to APT's Building Supply inventory.

The other 50% of APT's inventory belongs to the Disposable Protective Apparel segment. A large portion of this inventory consists of face masks, which saw a considerable rise in demand during the COVID-19 pandemic. This rise in demand correlated with a large build-up and subsequent tapering of inventory, as can be seen in the below table, starting at the peak year of 2021.

Year DPA Inventory (millions)
2021 $16.24
2022 $14.39
2023 $13.16
2024 $11.8
2025 (3Q) $11.58

This means that APT has been successful in selling off their DPA inventory since the COVID-19 peak without having to resort to inventory write downs which would indicate obsolescence. Profitability has remained stable during this time as well, indicating that APT did not have to significantly discount its inventory to sell it. This makes sense, as the DPA portion of APT's inventory, like the Building Supply segment, is made up of non-perishable items such as face masks and coveralls, which are insulated from both fickle consumer tastes and physical spoilage. An 80% discount to this inventory segment seems reasonable as well.

APT does not seem to have had any previous issues in turning their accounts receivable into cash. Indeed, according to their full year 2024 10-k, their allowance for doubtful accounts sat at $35,000 against an accounts receivable figure of $4.89 million, a rate of less than 1%. Regardless, to remain conservative, we will discount this account by 10%. We will also disregard the prepaid expense account entirely. Let's revisit our valuation above after applying these discounts.

Account Value (millions)
Cash/Equivalents $17.7(1) = $17.7
Inventory $23.5(0.8) = $18.8
Receivables $7.1(0.9) = $6.39
Prepaid Expenses $3.5(0) = $0
Sum $42.89 million

Taking our current asset value of $42.89 million and subtracting total liabilities of $11.3 million gives us $31.59 million. Dividing this number by shares outstanding of 10.4 million gives us an NCAV per share of roughly $3.05. Again, APT has not traded this cheaply since 2017.

Of course, this valuation has to be taken into the context of both the realities of the business and the alternatives the prospective investor has available to him. The NCAV method is an inherently conservative assessment of liquidation value, as it pays no heed to long-term assets such as property, plant, and equipment, or long-term investments. But the chances of APT, a healthy and profitable enterprise, liquidating its assets anytime soon are decidedly low. Furthermore, the company's management has been aggressively buying back shares, something that may cause an investor like Mohnish Pabrai to dub it a "cannibal".

These positive traits could act as an additional margin of safety for the company. In other words, the investor could probably justify purchasing shares of APT at a premium to its liquidation value, given the financial health of the firm and the evident willingness of management to return capital to shareholders.

It also needs to be noted that APT does not have any debt on its balance sheet. In fact, $8.2 million of the $11.3 million liabilities on APT's balance sheet are lease liabilities. Practically speaking, in the (unlikely) event of an actual liquidation, APT would probably not be required to pay back the entirety of its long-term lease obligations, as the landlord's claim to the remaining lease payments would likely be capped. It therefore might be reasonable to significantly discount this liability account on the balance sheet, which would meaningfully increase APT's NCAV value.

Let's unpack this further. Assuming APT decides to liquidate its assets, and further assuming, for argument's sake, that the amount of the lease liability the landlord can claim is consistent with bankruptcy code 502(b)(6), the landlord would be looking at a claim of 15% of the lease liability. In APT's case, this 15% would equate to $1.23 million, and would reduce total liabilities to about $4.3 million. Redoing the NCAV calculation, but without discounting any current assets, and discounting the lease liabilities account by 85%, gives us a much less conservative (and likely more realistic) NCAV of roughly $4.50.

But alas, the purpose of the NCAV valuation method is not to be precise, but to provide a sizable margin of safety to protect the investor against his own ignorance and miscalculations. As Buffett says, "It's better to be approximately right than precisely wrong". With this in mind, let's approximate the midpoint between our conservative and not-as-conservative valuations above and settle at a target price of $3.75. One could probably make a convincing argument to purchase shares above this price as well, but $3.75 and below would put this in the 'Strong Buy' category. For now, it's a tentative 'buy' at and below the current price of $4.50.

These ratings might change, however, based on the opportunity cost associated with favoring APT over alternative investments. If there are no other attractive investments available, buying APT at a loftier price might be a good allocation of capital, but if there are cheaper alternatives, waiting for APT to reach a cheaper price might be the preferred approach.

Risks

As the bulk of our valuation is based on APT's inventory, the primary risk to our investment thesis is this inventory being worth less than what we valued it at. This might happen for several reasons. If, for example, a building code changes that causes a significant portion of APT's roof underlays to become obsolete, or if the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NOSHA) abruptly changes a safety standard for APT's N-95 face masks, inventory write-downs would have to be recorded, diminishing book value and ultimately eroding the investor's margin of safety. This risk seems to be unlikely, however, as there has been no evidence of significant inventory impairments in the country's extensive history.

The investor should also keep a vigilant eye out for any telltale signs that might indicate a more subtle decline in inventory value, such as shrinking gross margins without a corresponding increase in the cost of goods sold. This might signal that inventory is being sold at a discount due to softening demand or price competition, which would also be evident in diminishing cash flows. Fortunately for the investor, APT has had reasonably stable gross margins over the past ten years, making any potential anomalies easy to detect and investigate in a proactive manner. Even in a worst-case scenario, if the investor manages to purchase shares at a low enough price, the margin of safety should be sufficient to protect against any downside that may otherwise occur.

Conclusion

APT is a financially sound company with reliable assets and potentially overstated liabilities. While it's important to remain as conservative as possible as a value investor, it is perhaps equally important to be reasonable. If we were to blindly apply Ben Graham's NNWC formula to account for APT's inventory-dominant current asset position, we would come up with a conservative, but probably unreasonably conservative, valuation considering the favorable qualities of the business. With a slightly more aggressive approach to valuation, the investor can land on a price target that is more realistic while remaining, on the whole, reasonably conservative. Buying at or below the identified price target should insulate the investor from any overwhelming downside if a worst-case scenario were to occur, while at the same time keeping open the possibility of capturing any upside that becomes manifest from the market recognizing the company's true value.
